items.front: When may radar services be provided?
items.back: Only when the controller is personally satisfied that the radar presentation and equipment performance are adequate for the service being provided.

items.front: Whose judgment controls whether the radar presentation is adequate for the service being provided?
items.back: The controller providing the service.

items.front: What must be done when radar malfunctions or unexpected outages occur?
items.back: Notify the OS/CIC and advise adjacent facilities when appropriate.

items.front: Are radar services limited to the distance and altitude parameters obtained during the commissioning flight check?
items.back: No.

items.front: What two factors must be adequate before radar service is provided?
items.back: The radar presentation and the equipment performance.

items.front: If the radar presentation becomes intermittent and no longer seems adequate, what is the correct response?
items.back: Do not continue treating it as adequate radar service and notify the OS/CIC of the malfunction.

items.front: When can a controller provide radar services?
items.back: Only when PERSONALLY satisfied that radar presentation and equipment performance is adequate. Per 5-1-1(a).

items.front: What surveillance sources should be used?
items.back: Approved ATC surveillance sources.

items.front: In terminal FUSION mode, when does the standard source-use restriction still apply?
items.back: Only when required by facility directive.

items.front: When may secondary-radar-only targets be used to conduct surveillance final approaches?
items.back: Only when an emergency exists and the pilot concurs.

items.front: What advisory describes a temporary primary-radar outage with secondary-only service availability?
items.back: Primary radar unavailable. Radar services available on transponder or ADS-B equipped aircraft only.

items.front: What terminal modes permit ADS-B and WAM targets to support all terminal services, including services for procedures annotated radar required?
items.back: STARS Fusion, STARS FMA, and STARS Multi-Sensor Mode.

items.front: What WAM limitation applies in the EAS?
items.back: WAM-derived targets cannot be used to provide 3 NM separation in the EAS.

items.front: When may the primary-radar-unavailable advisory be omitted?
items.back: When it is provided on ATIS or by other appropriate notice to pilots as allowed by the paragraph.

items.front: What surveillance sources must be used?
items.back: Approved ATC surveillance sources. TERMINAL FUSION mode exempts from 5-1-2a unless required by facility directive. Per 5-1-2.

items.front: Where are all EA activity requests referred?
items.back: The appropriate center supervisor.

items.front: If EA activity interferes with en route radar use, who is asked to suspend it?
items.back: The responsible military unit or aircraft, if the initial request was received directly from the pilot.

items.front: If EA activity interferes with terminal radar use, what is the normal path for requesting suspension?
items.back: Through the ARTCC.

items.front: When may a terminal facility broadcast a stop request directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency?
items.back: When immediate cessation of the activity is required.

items.front: Before a terminal facility broadcasts a resume clearance directly to the EA aircraft, what must be obtained?
items.back: ARTCC approval.

items.front: What items belong in each EA stop request?
items.back: The facility name, the type of EA activity, the radar band affected, and when feasible the expected duration of suspension.

items.front: What phraseology stops buzzer activity on a named band or channel?
items.back: Big Photo ... stop buzzer on (frequency band or channel).

items.front: Where are EA activity requests referred?
items.back: To the appropriate center supervisor. Per 5-1-3(a).

items.front: Which aircraft receive merging target procedures, except while established in a holding pattern?
items.back: Radar-identified aircraft at 10,000 feet and above, turbojet aircraft regardless of altitude, and presidential aircraft regardless of altitude.

items.front: When is traffic information issued under the merging target rule?
items.back: When covered aircraft targets appear likely to merge, unless they are separated by more than the appropriate vertical separation minima.

items.front: In RVSM airspace, what extra condition triggers vectoring to prevent targets from merging when the aircraft are 1,000 feet apart?
items.back: Either pilot reports being unable RVSM due to turbulence or mountain wave.

items.front: If the pilot requests a vector to avoid merging targets, what should ATC do?
items.back: Vector the aircraft if able and inform the pilot if unable to provide vector service.

items.front: Why should traffic be issued early in a merging-target situation?
items.back: Because aircraft closure rates can be rapid, so the pilot needs time to decide whether a vector is necessary.

items.front: What phraseology may be used to issue a vector for merging traffic avoidance?
items.back: Fly heading two niner zero, vector for traffic.

items.front: What aircraft get merging target procedures?
items.back:
1. All aircraft at 10,000 feet and above.
2. ALL turbojets regardless of altitude.
Exception: aircraft in holding patterns. Per 5-1-4(a)(1-2).

items.front: When must radar surveillance of an outer-fix holding pattern airspace area be provided?
items.back: Whenever aircraft are holding there and the area, or a portion of it, is shown on the radar scope.

items.front: What type of holding area does this rule address?
items.back: Outer-fix holding pattern airspace areas or portions of them shown on the radar scope.

items.front: What should the controller attempt to detect while aircraft are holding there?
items.back: Any aircraft that stray outside the area.

items.front: If an aircraft is detected straying outside the holding pattern airspace area, what should ATC do?
items.back: Assist it to return to the assigned airspace.

items.front: Does this surveillance rule apply to portions of the holding area shown on the scope, or only the full pattern?
items.back: It applies to the area or any portions of it that are shown on the scope.

items.front: What surveillance is required when aircraft are holding?
items.back: Radar surveillance of outer fix holding pattern airspace. Detect and assist any aircraft straying outside. Per 5-1-5.

items.front: What do you do if an aircraft strays outside the holding area?
items.back: Assist the aircraft to return to the assigned protected airspace. Per 5-1-5.

items.front: When is a deviation advisory issued?
items.back: When the aircraft is observed in a position and on a track that will obviously cause it to deviate from protected airspace.

items.front: If necessary, what should ATC do after issuing a deviation advisory?
items.back: Help the aircraft return to the assigned protected airspace.

items.front: What is the total width of an RNAV ATS route described in the note?
items.back: 8 miles.

items.front: How much laterally protected airspace exists on each side of an RNAV ATS route centerline?
items.back: 4 miles on each side.

items.front: What route-centerline performance are aircraft on RNAV ATS routes expected to maintain?
items.back: Within 2 miles of the route centerline.

items.front: What may be indicated by an aircraft approaching the 2-mile RNAV centerline limit?
items.back: A navigation system error or failure.

items.front: When must a fix time be manually recorded?
items.back: At least once for each controlled aircraft in the sector when the EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational.

items.front: What type of operation does this manual fix posting rule address?
items.back: En route operations.

items.front: What aircraft does this rule apply to?
items.back: Controlled aircraft in the controller's sector of responsibility.

items.front: What system condition triggers this manual fix posting requirement?
items.back: The EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational.

items.front: How often must the observed or reported fix time be manually recorded for each controlled aircraft when this rule is active?
items.back: At least once.

items.front: When is manual fix posting required?
items.back: At least once for each controlled aircraft when EAS FDP flight progress recording is not operational. Per 5-1-7.

items.front: When may ATC request an estimate or report over a specific fix?
items.back: When necessary.

items.front: After ATC states "radar contact," what happens to compulsory reporting over reporting points?
items.back: It is discontinued.

items.front: When does normal position reporting resume after radar contact has been established?
items.back: When ATC informs the aircraft "radar contact lost" or "radar service terminated."

items.front: Name one published position-report request from this paragraph.
items.back: Over/passing (fix), miles from (fix), miles direction of (fix/airway/location), crossing/joining/departing (airway or route), or intercepting/crossing a named NAVAID radial.

items.front: Does radar contact permanently bar the controller from requesting a specific fix report?
items.back: No.

items.front: What statement from ATC causes compulsory position reporting to stop?
items.back: "Radar contact."

items.front: How does radar contact affect position reporting?
items.back: Aircraft discontinues compulsory reporting. Resumes when ATC states 'radar contact lost' or 'radar service terminated.' Per 5-1-8.

items.front: What must generally be done when radar service is terminated?
items.back: Inform the aircraft that radar service is terminated.

items.front: What phraseology is used to terminate radar service?
items.back: Radar service terminated. Add nonradar routing if required.

items.front: Name one situation in which radar service is automatically terminated and the aircraft need not be separately advised.
items.back: Cancellation of IFR outside the listed radar-service areas, or an aircraft on an instrument/visual/contact approach has landed or has been instructed to change to advisory frequency.

items.front: At a tower-controlled airport where radar coverage does not exist to within one-half mile of the runway end, what must be done for arriving aircraft?
items.back: They must be informed when radar service is terminated.

items.front: Does IFR cancellation inside Class B, Class C, TRSA, or where basic radar service is provided automatically terminate radar service without further advisory?
items.back: No.

items.front: If an aircraft has been instructed to change to advisory frequency while conducting an instrument, visual, or contact approach, what happens to radar service?
items.back: It is automatically terminated.

items.front: What must you do when radar service ends?
items.back: Inform the aircraft that radar service is terminated. Per 5-1-9(a).

items.front: Who decides whether a radar approach and landing are authorized under the weather minima?
items.back: The pilot remains responsible for determining whether the approach and landing are authorized under the applicable weather minima.

items.front: When may a radar approach be given?
items.back: To any aircraft upon request. May be OFFERED to aircraft in distress regardless of weather, or to expedite traffic. Per 5-10-1(b).

items.front: What transmission-acknowledgment instruction is used after contact with the final controller is established?
items.back: Tell the aircraft, while on the final approach course, not to acknowledge further transmissions.

items.front: When must a specific missed-approach procedure be issued on a radar approach?
items.back: Before final descent for a full-stop landing whenever any part of final approach will be conducted in IFR conditions.

items.front: When are climb-out or departure instructions issued for a low approach or touch-and-go?
items.back: Issue them before final descent begins, and include a specific heading and altitude unless the aircraft will maintain VFR and contact the tower.

items.front: What must climb-out instructions include?
items.back: A specific heading and altitude — except when aircraft will maintain VFR and contact tower. Per 5-10-12.

items.front: What must the final controller obtain from the tower for an aircraft on final to a towered airport?
items.back: Obtain the landing, touch-and-go, or low-approach clearance, then pass that clearance and the surface wind to the aircraft.

items.front: What if a safe radar approach becomes questionable because of final-approach abnormalities?
items.back: If the runway environment is not in sight, instruct the aircraft to execute the missed approach already issued or climb to a specified altitude and fly a specified course.

items.front: When may a military single-frequency approach aircraft be required to change frequency after the approach begins?
items.back: Only for the listed exceptions, such as completion of landing or low approach, daylight VFR, pilot request, emergency, visual approach, or IFR cancellation.

items.front: What core information must be issued before a radar approach?
items.back: Issue the altimeter setting and the other listed approach-information items, including applicable weather, before the radar approach begins.

items.front: What turn instruction is required on a no-gyro approach after turn-on to final and before the gate?
items.back: Instruct the aircraft to make half-standard rate turns.

items.front: When should lost-communications instructions be issued for a radar approach likely to encounter IFR weather?
items.back: Issue them as soon as possible after radar identification and radio communications are established, subject to the repeated-approach exception.

items.front: What if radar contact is lost before final approach starts?
items.back: Clear the aircraft to an appropriate NAVAID or fix for an instrument approach.

items.front: What if radar contact is lost during approach?
items.back: Before starting final: clear to NAVAID/fix for instrument approach. After starting final: different procedures apply. Per 5-10-5.

items.front: When is the landing check advisory issued on a normal radar-approach pattern?
items.back: Issue it on downwind in time for the pilot to complete it before turning base.

items.front: When is landing check issued for USA/USN?
items.back: On downwind leg, in time to complete before turning base. Per 5-10-6.

items.front: How often must an aircraft receive position information before final approach begins?
items.back: At least once before final approach starts.

items.front: What must be included when changing an aircraft to the final controller frequency?
items.back: Include the name of the facility with the frequency change instruction.

items.front: What is asked on initial contact with the final controller?
items.back: Request a communications check from the aircraft.

items.front: When are recommended altitudes provided on a surveillance approach?
items.back: Provide them only if the pilot requests them, and advise that recommended altitudes will be given for each mile on final down to MDA or circling MDA.

items.front: When are recommended altitudes given on final?
items.back: If the pilot requests. They are at or above published MDA for each mile on final. Per 5-11-1.

items.front: What visual-reference reports may be requested on a surveillance approach?
items.back: You may request the runway, approach lights, runway lights, or airport in sight, or for point-in-space operations ask when the pilot can proceed visually to the landing area.

items.front: What extra information is needed when a surveillance approach will end in a circle-to-land maneuver?
items.back: Obtain the aircraft approach category and then issue the applicable circling MDA before authorizing final descent.

items.front: What descent instruction is used on a surveillance approach when no descent restriction exists?
items.back: At the descent point, tell the aircraft to descend to its MDA.

items.front: How often should transmissions occur on surveillance final approach guidance?
items.back: Transmit approximately every 15 seconds while providing course guidance and deviation information.

items.front: How often to transmit on surveillance final?
items.back: Approximately every 15 seconds. Issue course guidance, on-course notification, and deviation information. Per 5-11-5(a).

items.front: When must surveillance approach guidance be discontinued?
items.back: Stop it when requested by the pilot, when safe continuation to the MAP is questionable, or when the aircraft is over the MAP.

items.front: When discontinue surveillance approach guidance?
items.back:
1. Pilot requests it
2. Continuation of safe approach to MAP is questionable. Per 5-11-6(a)(1-2).

items.front: When should glidepath notification be given on a PAR approach?
items.back: Advise the aircraft it is approaching glidepath about 10 to 30 seconds before final descent.

items.front: When inform aircraft approaching glidepath?
items.back: Approximately 10 to 30 seconds before final descent. Per 5-12-1.

items.front: What happens if the elevation portion of PAR equipment fails during a precision approach?
items.back: Stop normal PAR guidance and tell the aircraft to proceed visually or, if unable, execute the missed approach. If available, convert to an authorized surveillance approach.

items.front: What if PAR elevation fails?
items.back: Discontinue PAR instructions. Tell aircraft to take over visually, or if unable, execute missed approach. Per 5-12-10(a)(1).

items.front: When may a PAR approach continue if the ASR is unusable?
items.back: It may continue if a nonradar instrument approach positions the aircraft within PAR coverage or an adjacent radar facility can give a direct handoff to the PAR controller.

items.front: When is decision height provided on a PAR approach?
items.back: Provide the decision height whenever the pilot requests it.

items.front: What descent instruction is issued when a PAR aircraft reaches the point where final descent should start?
items.back: Issue BEGIN DESCENT.

items.front: How often should transmissions occur on a precision final approach?
items.back: Transmit approximately every 5 seconds while providing glidepath and course guidance.

items.front: How often to transmit on precision final?
items.back: Approximately every 5 seconds — much more frequent than the 15-second surveillance interval. Per 5-12-4(a).

items.front: How often must distance from touchdown be given on a PAR approach?
items.back: Advise the aircraft of its distance from touchdown at least once each mile on final approach.

items.front: What call is made when a PAR aircraft reaches decision height?
items.back: Advise the aircraft that it is at decision height.

items.front: How long is glidepath and course information continued on a PAR approach?
items.back: Continue it until the aircraft passes over the threshold, and below decision height it is advisory only.

items.front: When must precision approach guidance be discontinued?
items.back: Stop it if the pilot requests it, safe continuation is questionable, the aircraft passes the threshold, or the pilot reports the runway or lights in sight and proceeds visually.

items.front: When discontinue precision approach guidance?
items.back:
1. Pilot requests
2. Safe continuation questionable
3. Aircraft passes threshold
4. Pilot reports visual and requests termination. Per 5-12-8(a)(1-4).

items.front: When should after-landing communications transfer instructions be issued on a PAR approach?
items.back: Delay them slightly until the aircraft is on the landing rollout so the pilot is not distracted during touchdown.

items.front: What action is required for a CA or MCI alert?
items.back: Evaluate the reason for the alert WITHOUT DELAY and take appropriate action. Per 5-13-1(a).

items.front: What does CA stand for?
items.back: Conflict Alert. Per 5-13-1.

items.front: What does MCI stand for?
items.back: Mode C Intruder. Per 5-13-1.

items.front: Can a controller delay evaluating a CA alert if busy?
items.back: No — the evaluation must be done WITHOUT DELAY regardless of workload. Per 5-13-1(a).

items.front: What action for CA or MCI alert?
items.back: Evaluate the reason WITHOUT DELAY and take appropriate action. Per 5-13-1(a).

items.front: What must controllers do when SAA DOWN is displayed?
items.back: Manually create visual indicators of Special Activity Airspace status that impact their area of control responsibility. Per 5-13-10.

items.front: What when SAA DOWN is displayed?
items.back: Manually create visual indicators of SAA status. Per 5-13-10.

items.front: What is E-MSAW?
items.back: En Route Minimum Safe Altitude Warning. Per 5-13-2.

items.front: What action is required for an E-MSAW alert?
items.back: IMMEDIATELY analyze the situation and take the appropriate action to resolve the alert. Per 5-13-2(a).

items.front: How quickly must an E-MSAW alert be addressed?
items.back: IMMEDIATELY — the analysis and action must begin without delay. Per 5-13-2(a).

items.front: What is E-MSAW and required action?
items.back: En Route Minimum Safe Altitude Warning. IMMEDIATELY analyze and take action to resolve. Per 5-13-2(a).

items.front: What must the altitude field of the data block always reflect?
items.back: The CURRENT status of the aircraft unless otherwise specified in a facility directive. Per 5-13-3(a)(1).

items.front: When may assigned or interim altitude be modified?
items.back: Only when specified in a facility directive or letter of agreement. Do NOT modify otherwise. Per 5-13-3(a)(2).

items.front: What must altitude field always reflect?
items.back: The CURRENT status of the aircraft unless otherwise specified in a facility directive. Per 5-13-3(a)(1).

items.front: When are reported altitudes entered into the computer?
items.back: Whenever Mode C altitude information is either not available or is unreliable. Per 5-13-4.

items.front: What substitutes for Mode C altitude when unavailable?
items.back: Pilot-reported altitudes, entered into the computer by the controller. Per 5-13-4.

items.front: When are reported altitudes entered?
items.back: Whenever Mode C altitude is not available or is unreliable. Per 5-13-4.

items.front: Why must sectors display Mode C targets and data blocks?
items.back: The display is necessary for the application of Merging Target Procedures. Sectors must enter altitude limits and display filters to include the altitude stratum. Per 5-13-5.

items.front: Why display Mode C targets and data blocks?
items.back: Necessary for Merging Target Procedures. Enter altitude limits and display filters. Per 5-13-5.

items.front: When can the OK function override sector eligibility?
items.back: Only when: (a) prior coordination is effected, OR (b) the flight is within the control jurisdiction of the sector. Per 5-13-6(a-b).

items.front: When can OK function override sector eligibility?
items.back: Only when prior coordination is effected OR flight is within sector control jurisdiction. Per 5-13-6(a-b).

items.front: Can coast tracks be used for separation?
items.back: NO. Do not use coast tracks in the application of either radar or nonradar separation criteria. Per 5-13-7.

items.front: What is the critical restriction on coast tracks?
items.back: They must never be used for any separation — radar or nonradar. Per 5-13-7.

items.front: In what mode must coast tracks be initiated?
items.back: Only in Flight Plan Aided Tracking (FLAT) mode. Exception: free coast tracking may be used as a reminder for aircraft without stored flight plan information. Per 5-13-8(a).

items.front: What is the limited exception to the FLAT-mode requirement?
items.back: Free coast tracking may serve as a reminder that aircraft without computer-stored flight plan information are under your control. Per 5-13-8(a).

items.front: What mode for coast track initiation?
items.back: Only FLAT (Flight Plan Aided Tracking) mode. Free coast tracking may be used as a reminder. Per 5-13-8(a).

items.front: What four actions are required for ATC-initiated holding?
items.back:
1. Enter a hold message
2. Maintain a paired track
3. Enter EFC time via hold message, Hold Data Menu, or Hold View
4. Enter non-published hold details. Per 5-13-9(a)(1-4).

items.front: What four actions for ATC-initiated holding?
items.back:
1. Enter hold message
2. Maintain paired track
3. Enter EFC time
4. Enter non-published hold details. Per 5-13-9(a)(1-4).

items.front: What is STARS used for in terminal?
items.back: Identifying aircraft assigned a discrete beacon code, maintaining target identity, and performing handoffs between controllers. Per 5-14-1.

items.front: Do STARS procedures apply to FUSION targets?
items.back: Yes — all terminal-domain STARS procedures apply to the FUSION target. Per 5-14-1.

items.front: What is STARS used for?
items.back: Identifying aircraft with discrete beacon codes, maintaining target identity, and performing handoffs. Per 5-14-1.

items.front: Does terminal automation relieve the controller of responsibility?
items.back: NO. The controller retains responsibility for proper identification, identity maintenance, correct handoff, and separation of aircraft. Per 5-14-2.

items.front: Does terminal automation relieve controller responsibility?
items.back: NO. Controller retains responsibility for ID, identity, handoff, and separation. Per 5-14-2.

items.front: What four functions may terminal automation perform?
items.back:
1. Tracking
2. Tagging
3. Handoff
4. Altitude information
Per 5-14-3(a-d).

items.front: What four terminal automation functions?
items.back:
1. Tracking
2. Tagging
3. Handoff
4. Altitude information. Per 5-14-3(a-d).

items.front: How must terminal automation systems be used?
items.back: In accordance with this order and as specified in facility directives. Per 5-14-4.

items.front: How to use terminal automation systems?
items.back: Per this order and facility directives. Per 5-14-4.

items.front: What aircraft designators must be used in data blocks?
items.back: Two-letter ICAO designators or three-letter designators as appropriate. Single letter alpha prefix only when program limitations dictate. Per 5-14-5(a).

items.front: What designators must be used in data blocks?
items.back: Two-letter ICAO or three-letter designators. Single letter alpha prefix only when program limitations dictate. Per 5-14-5(a).

items.front: What is the terminal equivalent of 5-13-1?
items.back: Same requirement: evaluate the reason for CA or MCI alert without delay and take appropriate action. Per 5-14-6(a).

items.front: What action on terminal CA/MCI alert?
items.back: Evaluate reason without delay and take appropriate action. Per 5-14-6(a).

items.front: For which aircraft is MSAW processing inhibited?
items.back: VFR aircraft and aircraft that cancel IFR flight plans — unless the pilot specifically requests otherwise. Per 5-14-7(a).

items.front: Can a VFR pilot request MSAW processing?
items.back: Yes — MSAW is inhibited by default for VFR but can be activated if the pilot specifically requests it. Per 5-14-7(a).

items.front: Which aircraft have MSAW inhibited?
items.back: VFR aircraft and IFR-canceling aircraft — unless pilot specifically requests otherwise. Per 5-14-7(a).

items.front: When may the track suspend function be used?
items.back: Only when data block overlap in holding patterns or near final approach creates an unworkable situation. Suspend non-altitude-readout tracks first. Per 5-14-8.

items.front: Which tracks must be suspended first?
items.back: Those NOT displaying automatic altitude readouts. If the condition persists, those displaying altitude readouts may also be suspended. Per 5-14-8.

items.front: When may track suspend be used?
items.back: Only when data block overlap creates unworkable situation. Suspend non-altitude-readout tracks first. Per 5-14-8.

items.front: What is ARV and what action does it require?
items.back: Approach Runway Verification. Evaluate the reason and take appropriate action. Acknowledgment signifies action has been taken. Per 5-14-9(a-c).

items.front: What is ARV and required action?
items.back: Approach Runway Verification. Evaluate and take action. Acknowledgment signifies action taken. Per 5-14-9(a-c).

items.front: What common military/civil mode is designated for ATC use?
items.back: Mode 3/A.

items.front: Which aircraft should receive beacon code assignments?
items.back: Only aircraft equipped with ADS-B and/or a transponder.

items.front: Are ADS-B-equipped aircraft still required to have an operable transponder?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What beacon codes should normally be issued unless another rule, directive, or LOA says otherwise?
items.back: Computer-assigned beacon codes.

items.front: What code source is used for aircraft that will remain within the terminal facility's delegated airspace?
items.back: A code from the subset allocated to that terminal facility.

items.front: What code source is normally used for terminal aircraft that will enter an adjacent facility's delegated airspace?
items.back: A beacon code assigned by the ARTCC computer.

items.front: Why is an ARTCC computer-assigned code used for aircraft entering adjacent delegated airspace?
items.back: It gives the adjacent facility advance information and supports auto-acquisition before handoff.

items.front: When is Code 4000 assigned?
items.back: When the flight involves frequent or rapid altitude changes in more than one stratum or another category not compatible with a discrete code assignment.

items.front: What mode is the common military/civil ATC mode?
items.back: Mode 3/A. Assign beacon codes only to ADS-B and/or transponder-equipped aircraft. Per 5-2-1(a)(1-2).

items.front: When may you instruct an aircraft to squawk standby?
items.back: Use standby only when the aircraft is about 15 miles from destination and you no longer need the transponder or ADS-B, or when needed to reduce clutter, then return it to normal as soon as practical.

items.front: When can transponder go to standby?
items.back:
1. Approximately 15 miles from destination.
2. To reduce clutter in multi-target areas (instruct return to normal ASAP). Per 5-2-10(a-b).

items.front: Which codes must be continuously monitored in normal code-monitor duty?
items.back: Monitor assigned codes plus 1200, 1202, 1203, 1255, and 1277 unless your area is only Class A. Monitor 4000 and any substitute code when nearby special-use operations require it.

items.front: What five non-discrete codes must be monitored?
items.back: 1200, 1202, 1203, 1255, and 1277 — unless your area is Class A only. Per 5-2-11(a).

items.front: What do you do when an aircraft is not displaying its assigned code or the transponder appears failed?
items.back: Tell the pilot to reset the transponder and squawk the assigned code, and notify the next control position or facility if the transponder is inoperative or malfunctioning.

items.front: What must you tell aircraft when the beacon interrogator appears inoperative or malfunctioning?
items.back: Advise the affected aircraft that the beacon interrogator is inoperative or malfunctioning.

items.front: When may you disapprove or withdraw approval to operate with a failed transponder or ADS-B Out transmitter?
items.back: Only on the basis of traffic conditions or other operational factors.

items.front: When must Mode C altitude readout be validated?
items.back: Validate it after initial track start, track start from coast or frozen status, during and after an unreliable readout, and after interfacility handoff except for the listed tower or TRACON exceptions.

items.front: When must Mode C altitude be validated?
items.back:
1. After initial track start
2. After coast/frozen track start
3. During and after handoff
4. After code change. Per 5-2-15(a).

items.front: When is assigned altitude verification waived for Mode C aircraft on initial contact?
items.back: It is waived if the pilot states the altitude, you assign a new one while the aircraft is climbing or descending, valid Mode C shows established at it, or the terminal intrafacility exception applies.

items.front: When must altitude be confirmed on initial contact?
items.back: Always — unless the pilot reports leaving an altitude and the Mode C readout is valid. Per 5-2-16.

items.front: What altitude-confirmation rule applies to non-Mode C aircraft?
items.back: On initial contact, verify assigned altitude unless a listed exception applies. In the USA, reconfirm all pilot altitude readbacks.

items.front: How do you control automatic altitude reporting?
items.back: Tell the pilot when altitude reporting should be on or off by issuing SQUAWK ALTITUDE or STOP ALTITUDE SQUAWK.

items.front: When may an inflight deviation from transponder or Mode C requirements be approved?
items.back: Between 10,000 and 18,000 feet MSL in the contiguous U.S., do not approve an inflight deviation for aircraft without installed equipment except in an emergency.

items.front: What airspace for Mode C deviation requests?
items.back: 10,000-18,000 MSL in the 48 contiguous states and DC, excluding at/below 2,500 AGL. Per 5-2-19.

items.front: When may you normally request a change from the transferring facility's beacon code?
items.back: After the aircraft is within your area of responsibility, unless a directive, LOA, or handoff coordination says otherwise.

items.front: What does this paragraph specifically regulate?
items.back: Requests to change the code the aircraft was squawking in the transferring facility's area.

items.front: What can authorize an earlier beacon code change before the aircraft enters your area?
items.back: A directive, a letter of agreement, or coordination at the time of handoff.

items.front: Does accepting a handoff by itself authorize an immediate code change while the aircraft is still outside your area?
items.back: No.

items.front: If no exception applies and the aircraft is still outside your area, what should you do about its code?
items.back: Leave it on the transferring facility's code until it is within your area of responsibility.

items.front: When can a code change be requested?
items.back: Not until the aircraft is within your area of responsibility unless otherwise coordinated. Per 5-2-2.

items.front: How is beacon termination accomplished?
items.back: Instruct the pilot to STOP SQUAWK. For military aircraft, specify the mode if needed.

items.front: How should terminal altitude filters be set?
items.back: Set filters to cover all altitudes in the controller's jurisdiction, with the upper limit at least 1,000 feet above the highest altitude responsibility.

items.front: How are altitude filter limits set?
items.back: Upper: no lower than 1,000 ft above highest responsibility. Lower: 1,000 ft or more below lowest responsibility. Per 5-2-21.

items.front: What must be done when an aircraft's ADS-B transmitter appears inoperative or malfunctioning?
items.back: Notify the OS/CIC of the call sign, location, and UTC time. Inform the pilot except for DoD or authorized OFF operations, and stop transmissions if the malfunction affects safe ATC operations.

items.front: What triggers a CSMM ADS-B alert and what do you do with it?
items.back: It occurs when the transmitted ADS-B flight ID does not match the flight plan aircraft identification, and you inform the aircraft of the mismatch.

items.front: What do you do if a pilot requests ADS-B Out OFF operations?
items.back: Do not approve the request. Notify the OS/CIC with the aircraft call sign and location, and do not tell an authorized OFF aircraft that its ADS-B appears inoperative.

items.front: When is Code 7700 assigned?
items.back: When the pilot declares an emergency and the aircraft is not radar identified.

items.front: What phraseology is used to assign 7700 to a non-radar-identified emergency aircraft?
items.back: Squawk MAYDAY on 7700.

items.front: What does ERAM display instead of 7700?
items.back: EMRG.

items.front: What do STARS and MEARTS display instead of 7700?
items.back: EM.

items.front: After radio and radar contact are established, when may ATC request a change from 7700 to a discrete code?
items.back: When feasible, for aircraft other than single-piloted helicopters and single-piloted turbojet aircraft, considering pilot concurrence, the emergency, and flight conditions.

items.front: Which aircraft may be unable to change transponder settings during an emergency?
items.back: Single-piloted helicopters and single-piloted turbojet aircraft.

items.front: What must a terminal facility do for a Mode C equipped VFR emergency aircraft that no longer requires 7700?
items.back: Assign a beacon code that permits terminal MSAW alarm processing.

items.front: What must an en route controller do for a Mode C equipped VFR emergency aircraft that no longer requires 7700?
items.back: Make the appropriate keyboard entry to ensure en route MSAW alarm processing.

items.front: What emergency transponder codes exist?
items.back:
7700 = Emergency (not radar identified)
7600 = Radio Failure
7500 = Hijack
7400 = UAS Lost Link
Per 5-2-3 through 5-2-6.

items.front: What does Code 7600 indicate for controller handling?
items.back: A communications-failure event.

items.front: What must you do when you observe Code 7600?
items.back: Apply communications-failure procedures.

items.front: What can be expected from an aircraft that loses two-way radio communications capability?
items.back: It can be expected to squawk 7600.

items.front: What does ERAM display instead of 7600?
items.back: RDOF.

items.front: What do STARS and MEARTS display instead of 7600?
items.back: RF.

items.front: What does Code 7600 indicate?
items.back: Radio failure — apply procedures in paragraph 10-4-4. Per 5-2-4.

items.front: What does Code 7500 indicate for controller handling?
items.back: A hijack or unlawful-interference event.

items.front: What must you do when you observe Code 7500?
items.back: Apply hijacked-aircraft procedures.

items.front: What does ERAM display instead of 7500?
items.back: HIJK.

items.front: What do STARS and MEARTS display instead of 7500?
items.back: HJ.

items.front: Does Code 7500 get handled the same way as a 7600 communications-failure display?
items.back: No.

items.front: What does Code 7500 indicate?
items.back: Hijack or unlawful interference — apply procedures in paragraph 10-2-6. Per 5-2-5.

items.front: What does Code 7400 indicate when transmitted by a UAS?
items.back: The control link between the aircraft and the pilot is lost.

items.front: When a UAS displays 7400, what should you determine first?
items.back: The lost link procedure outlined in the Special Airworthiness Certificate or the Certificate of Waiver or Authorization.

items.front: What coordination action may be required after a 7400 display?
items.back: Coordinate as required to allow the UAS to execute the lost link procedure.

items.front: When feasible, who should be advised after a UAS lost-link event is observed?
items.back: The OS/CIC.

items.front: What minimum items should be available in the lost link procedure?
items.back: Lost link route of flight, orbit points, altitudes, communications procedures, and preplanned termination points if recovery is infeasible.

items.front: Do all UAS airframes squawk 7400 during lost link?
items.back: No. Some may continue to squawk 7600, and the same lost-link procedures still apply.

items.front: If the UAS deviates from the programmed lost link procedure or another anomaly develops, how should the event be treated?
items.back: As an abnormal or emergency situation rather than routine lost-link execution alone.

items.front: What does Code 7400 indicate?
items.back: UAS lost link — control link between aircraft and pilot is lost. Lost link procedures are pre-programmed. Per 5-2-6.

items.front: What code should a VFR aircraft use when radar advisory service ends?
items.back: Instruct the aircraft to squawk VFR or 1200. VFR aircraft receiving radar advisories should have a computer-assigned beacon code while the service is being provided.

items.front: How are pressure-suit flights and flights above FL600 normally handled on beacon code?
items.back: Keep them on the reserved special-use code filed in the flight plan. Change, standby, or stop-squawk instructions are used only if same-code conflicts make them necessary.

items.front: What code rule applies to exercise FAKER aircraft?
items.back: Ensure they remain on the exercise discrete beacon code filed in the flight plan.

items.front: What must happen before radar service is provided?
items.back: Establish and maintain radar identification of the aircraft before providing radar service, unless a specific exception applies.

items.front: What is the prerequisite for radar service?
items.back: Establish and maintain radar identification of the aircraft involved. Per 5-3-1.

items.front: What are the main primary-radar identification methods?
items.back: Identify by observing a departure within 1 mile of the runway with required coordination, correlating a direct position report, or observing an identifying turn under the listed conditions.

items.front: How is a departing aircraft identified by primary radar?
items.back: Target within 1 mile of the takeoff runway end at airports with a tower, with verbal or nonverbal rolling/boundary notification for each departure. Per 5-3-2(a).

items.front: What methods may be used for beacon or ADS-B identification?
items.back: Use IDENT, observe a code change, observe standby then normal target response, or use the approved en route automated association of a discrete code and target symbol.

items.front: What is the IDENT method for beacon identification?
items.back: Request the pilot to activate the IDENT feature on the transponder. Per 5-3-3(a).

items.front: When is an auto-acquired target considered identified in terminal automation?
items.back: When the data block is visible and confirmed by radar or beacon ID procedures or by a qualifying NAS handoff, and the block is not in a disallowed status.

items.front: What must you do if target identification is questionable?
items.back: Use more than one identification method when doubt exists. If identification is questionable, immediately reidentify the aircraft or terminate radar service.

items.front: When use multiple ID methods?
items.back: When proximity of targets, duplication of observed action, or any other doubt exists. Per 5-3-5(a).

items.front: When is position information required after identification?
items.back: Give position information when identification was established by identifying turns or beacon methods. It is not required for position correlation or a departure identified within 1 mile of the runway end.

items.front: When are radar contact and radar contact lost advisories issued?
items.back: Advise radar contact when identification is first established or later reestablished, and advise radar contact lost when identification is lost.

items.front: When is 'radar contact' stated?
items.back:
1. When initial radar identification is established.
2. When identification is reestablished after loss or service termination. Per 5-3-7(a)(1-2).

items.front: What are the minimum en route data-block requirements?
items.back: Retain the data block until the aircraft and conflicts are clear. It must at least show flight identification and altitude, and ERAM paired tracks need a full data block when you own separation.

items.front: How long must en route data blocks be retained?
items.back: Until aircraft has exited the sector AND all potential conflicts are resolved. Must display flight ID and altitude as a minimum. Per 5-3-8.

items.front: When may a terminal data block be dropped?
items.back: Retain it until the aircraft and conflicts are clear. If delegated airspace extends beyond Class B or C, a VFR block may be dropped once the aircraft is clear and radar service has ended.

items.front: What is required when radar identification transfer is not operationally feasible?
items.back: Use an LOA or direct controller coordination for the specified period. In normal radar surveillance areas, interfacility and intrafacility transfers should still be accomplished.

items.front: Why transfer radar identification?
items.back: To provide continuous radar service and facilitate safe, orderly, and expeditious traffic flow. Per 5-4-1.

items.front: What are the main rules for en route fourth-line data block usage?
items.back: Use it only for authorized coordination entries on radar-identified aircraft, and remove any free text before starting identification transfer.

items.front: How do handoff, point out, and traffic coordination differ?
items.back: A handoff transfers radar identification and communications, a point out transfers radar identification only, and traffic transfers identification to coordinate separation restrictions.

items.front: What is a handoff?
items.back: The action to transfer radar identification from one controller to another when the aircraft will enter receiving controller's airspace and communications will be transferred. Per 5-4-2(a).

items.front: What methods may be used to transfer radar identification?
items.back: Use physical pointing, landline voice, automation, or terminal Modify or Quick Look procedures when specifically authorized by facility directive.

items.front: What are the responsibilities when using the term traffic to coordinate separation?
items.back: The controller issuing traffic states the restrictions, and the controller accepting them ensures approved separation is maintained.

items.front: What must the controller issuing 'traffic' provide?
items.back: Appropriate restrictions. The accepting controller must ensure approved separation is maintained. Per 5-4-4(a-b).

items.front: What must the transferring controller do during a handoff?
items.back: Complete the handoff before delegated-airspace entry, coordinate any post-handoff changes, pass pertinent info and restrictions, and resolve conflicts before communications transfer.

items.front: What changes require approval during pending handoff?
items.back: Flight path, altitude, speed, or data block information changes. Obtain receiving controller's verbal approval. Per 5-4-5(b).

items.front: What must the receiving controller do before accepting a handoff?
items.back: Ensure the target association is correct, issue any needed restrictions for safe entry, and comply with restrictions issued by the transferring controller unless otherwise coordinated.

items.front: What is required for a point out into another controller's delegated airspace?
items.back: Obtain approval before the aircraft enters that airspace, provide the required identification and altitude information, and advise when the point out is no longer needed.

items.front: What is a point out?
items.back: Obtaining approval before an aircraft enters receiving controller's delegated airspace WITHOUT transferring communications. EN ROUTE: revert to verbal if no response. Per 5-4-7(a)(1).

items.front: When may Automated Information Transfer be used without verbal coordination?
items.back: Only during radar handoff, through information shown in full data blocks, and under procedures specified in the facility's AIT directive or LOA.

items.front: What conditions enable AIT?
items.back:
1. During radar handoff
2. Via information displayed in full data blocks
3. Following facility AIT directive and/or LOA. Per 5-4-8(a-c).

items.front: What makes prearranged coordination valid?
items.back: It must be established and published in a facility directive. A controller may not let an aircraft enter another controller's airspace without proper coordination.

items.front: When must radar separation be applied to RNAV aircraft?
items.back: Apply radar separation to all RNAV aircraft at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace.

items.front: Where must radar separation be applied to RNAV aircraft?
items.back: To all RNAV aircraft at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace. Per 5-5-1(a).

items.front: Where must RNAV radar separation be applied?
items.back: To all RNAV aircraft at and below FL450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace. Per 5-5-1(a).

items.front: What is the main adjacent-airspace rule when radar coordination has not been completed?
items.back: Keep the aircraft separated from the adjacent-airspace boundary by the applicable minima, with larger minima when the adjoining airspace uses nonradar separation.

items.front: What edge-of-scope separation applies inside 40 miles from the antenna?
items.back: Use 3 miles from the edge of scope until nonradar separation is established.

items.front: What correction is required for specified beacon target displacement?
items.back: Add 1 mile to the applicable minimum when separating a displaced beacon target from a primary target, adjacent airspace, obstructions, or terrain.

items.front: How is target separation measured on radar displays?
items.back: Separate primary targets center-to-center, beacon slashes end-to-end, and on all-digital displays keep digital targets center-to-center without letting them touch.

items.front: How is radar separation applied between targets?
items.back: Between centers of primary targets. Between ends of beacon control slashes. Do NOT allow primary targets to touch. Per 5-5-2(a-c).

items.front: What does target resolution require besides keeping targets apart?
items.back: When target resolution is used, traffic advisories and safety alerts are mandatory.

items.front: What is the basic terminal radar-separation minimum in single-sensor ASR or DTAS inside 40 miles?
items.back: Use 3 miles inside 40 miles from the antenna. Beyond that, use 5 miles unless a listed special case applies.

items.front: For Table 5-5-2 wake turbulence on approach, when must the listed spacing exist?
items.back: At the time the preceding aircraft is over the landing threshold.

items.front: Under Table 5-5-2, what spacing applies to a Category H follower behind a Category C leader on approach?
items.back: 5 NM.

items.front: What are the basic radar separation minima?
items.back: TERMINAL single sensor ASR: 3 miles. EN ROUTE: 5 miles. Additional separation based on distance from antenna and equipment type. Per 5-5-4.

items.front: How may aircraft that are not laterally separated be vertically separated by radar?
items.back: Assign altitudes using valid Mode C and keep the required vertical minima at all times, or reuse an altitude only after the first aircraft has left it under the paragraph's standards.

items.front: When may Mode C not be used to provide vertical separation?
items.back: Do not use Mode C for vertical separation on a cruise clearance, a contact approach, or the other cases specifically barred by the paragraph.

items.front: When can passing or diverging separation replace other approved separation?
items.back: Use it only after the required crossing or passing geometry exists, the courses diverge by the required angle, and the targets are monitored to keep the required spacing increasing.

items.front: When can passing/diverging separation be applied?
items.back: When aircraft on opposite/reciprocal courses have been observed to pass each other, or when on diverging courses. Other approved separation may then be discontinued. Per 5-5-7(a)(1)(a).

items.front: What extra radar separation is required for standard formation flights?
items.back: Add 1 mile to the applicable radar minimum for one standard formation, and add 2 miles when separating two standard formations from each other.

items.front: What obstruction-separation minimum applies in terminal radar inside 40 miles?
items.back: Separate aircraft from prominent obstructions by 3 miles inside 40 miles from the antenna, unless a listed fusion or multisensor rule applies.

items.front: What are obstruction separation minima?
items.back: Less than 40 NM from antenna: 3 miles. 40 NM or more: 5 miles. Per 5-5-9(a)(1-2).

items.front: When may controllers vector aircraft in Class G airspace?
items.back: Only when the pilot requests it and only as an additional service.

items.front: When can aircraft be vectored in controlled airspace?
items.back: For separation, safety, noise abatement, operational advantage, confidence maneuver, or pilot request. Class G: only upon pilot request as additional service. Per 5-6-1(a-b).

items.front: When can aircraft be vectored?
items.back: In controlled airspace: for separation, safety, noise abatement, operational advantage, confidence maneuver, or pilot request. Class G: only upon pilot request as additional service. Per 5-6-1(a-b).

items.front: What must a vectoring instruction include?
items.back: Specify the heading or turn to be flown and, when the vector starts, tell the pilot the purpose and any relevant expectation for when guidance or service will end.

items.front: What two elements in a vector instruction?
items.back: Direction of turn (if appropriate) and magnetic heading to be flown. Per 5-6-2(a)(1).

items.front: When may a terminal controller vector below minimum IFR altitude?
items.back: Only under the listed obstacle-clearance conditions for departing IFR aircraft or missed approaches, as described in facility directives and the paragraph's lateral or climb protections.

items.front: What is the general rule for ATC speed adjustments?
items.back: Use the minimum speed control needed to achieve spacing, avoid unnecessary increases and decreases, and terminate speed control when it is no longer needed.

items.front: What speed adjustment pattern should be avoided?
items.back: Alternating decreases and increases. Keep adjustments to minimum necessary. Per 5-7-1.

items.front: What kinds of speed instructions may ATC issue?
items.back: ATC may assign present or specific speed, a speed at or above or below a value, maximum forward speed, slowest practical speed, or an increase or reduction to a stated speed or Mach number.

items.front: What arrival speed may be assigned below Class B airspace or in a VFR corridor through Class B?
items.back: Assign not more than 200 knots.

items.front: What is the minimum speed between FL280 and 10,000 feet?
items.back: 250 knots or the equivalent Mach number. Per 5-7-3(a).

items.front: When should ATC tell an aircraft to resume normal speed?
items.back: Use resume normal speed only when ATC-assigned speed control is no longer needed and no published speed restriction still applies.

items.front: How are speed adjustments terminated?
items.back: Advise aircraft to 'resume normal speed' when no longer needed and no published restrictions apply. Per 5-7-4(a).

items.front: What is the key rule when vectoring a departure on a radar SID?
items.back: Do not use a diverse vector area concurrently with a radar SID. If the SID says fly assigned heading, keep assignments within the published heading range until reaching MVA or MIA.

items.front: Can DVA be used with radar SID?
items.back: No — concurrent use is not permitted when vectoring on a radar SID. Per 5-8-1(a).

items.front: How should initial headings be assigned when a departure will be vectored immediately after takeoff?
items.back: Assign an initial heading consistent with the SID or the DVA, if applicable. A SID and a DVA cannot be used at the same time.

items.front: What conditions are required for successive or simultaneous departure minima?
items.back: Radar identification must be established within 1 mile of the departure point, and the courses must diverge by at least the required amount for the specific procedure used.

items.front: What enables successive/simultaneous departures?
items.back: Radar ID within 1 mile of departure end AND courses that will diverge. Per 5-8-3.

items.front: When may a departure be released with only 2 miles from an arrival on final?
items.back: Only when the separation will increase to at least 3 miles, or 5 miles beyond 40 miles from the antenna, within 1 minute after takeoff.

items.front: What separation between departure and arrival on same runway?
items.back: 2 miles minimum if separation will increase to 3 miles. 5 miles when 40 or more miles from the antenna. Per 5-8-4.

items.front: What divergence is required for simultaneous operations between a departure and an arrival on another parallel or diverging runway?
items.back: The departure course must diverge immediately by at least 30 degrees from the missed-approach course until separation is applied, plus the runway-spacing condition in the paragraph must be met.

items.front: When can simultaneous parallel departure/arrival ops be conducted?
items.back: When the departure course diverges IMMEDIATELY by at least 30 degrees from the missed approach course. Per 5-8-5.

items.front: When can parallel departure/arrival ops be simultaneous?
items.back: When the departure course diverges immediately by at least 30 degrees from the missed approach course. Per 5-8-5.

items.front: How far outside the approach gate should arriving aircraft normally be vectored to intercept final?
items.back: Normally at least 2 miles outside the approach gate. Closer vectoring is limited to the listed weather or pilot-request exceptions.

items.front: At what distance must final approach course be intercepted?
items.back: At least 2 miles outside the approach gate. May be reduced when ceiling is at least 500 ft above MVA/MIA and visibility is 3+ miles. Per 5-9-1(a).

items.front: What runway-centerline spacing defines widely spaced parallels for simultaneous independent approaches without final monitors?
items.back: More than 9,000 feet at field elevations of 5,000 feet MSL or below, or 9,200 feet above 5,000 feet MSL.

items.front: What is required if reduced-separation approaches lose their approved spacing because of a go-around or terminated approach?
items.back: Immediately issue control instructions that build approved separation, such as altitude or lateral divergence, before the reduced standard is lost.

items.front: What happens during go-around with reduced separation?
items.back: Control instructions must be EXPEDITIOUSLY issued before losing the approved reduced separation. Per 5-9-11.

items.front: What is the normal maximum final-approach interception angle 2 miles or more from the gate?
items.back: Do not exceed 30 degrees unless the table provides a different limit for the specific situation.

items.front: What maximum final approach course intercept angle applies less than 2 miles from the approach gate or during triple simultaneous approaches?
items.back: 20 degrees.

items.front: What maximum final approach course intercept angle normally applies 2 miles or more from the approach gate?
items.back: 30 degrees, with 45 degrees allowed for helicopters.

items.front: What must you tell an aircraft before vectoring it across the final approach course?
items.back: Advise that it should expect vectors across final and state the reason for doing so.

items.front: What arrival instructions must be issued before an aircraft reaches the approach gate?
items.back: Give position relative to a fix on final, a vector to intercept final if needed, and the approach clearance unless a radar approach is being conducted.

items.front: Who is normally responsible for separating radar arrivals on approach?
items.back: The radar controller performing the approach-control function, unless the tower provides visual separation or an LOA or directive authorizes otherwise.

items.front: Who is responsible for radar arrival separation?
items.back: The radar controller performing approach control — unless tower provides visual separation or LOA authorizes otherwise. Per 5-9-5(a).

items.front: What diagonal spacing is used for simultaneous dependent approaches with runway centerlines 2,500 to 3,600 feet apart?
items.back: Use 1 mile of diagonal radar separation between successive aircraft on adjacent final approach courses.

items.front: At what point may the diagonal minimum be used for simultaneous dependent approaches?
items.back: After aircraft are established on adjacent final approach courses; turn-on still requires 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar separation.

items.front: What are simultaneous dependent approach minima?
items.back: 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar between aircraft during turn-on. 1 mile radar diagonally between successive. Per 5-9-6(a)(1-2).

items.front: What are dependent approach minima?
items.back: 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar during turn-on. 1 mile diagonally between successive. Per 5-9-6(a)(1-2).

items.front: What runway-centerline spacing normally supports dual or triple simultaneous independent approaches?
items.back: They normally require parallel runway centerlines separated by at least 3,600 feet unless another authorized provision applies.

items.front: What are independent approach minima?
items.back: 1,000 feet vertical or 3 miles radar until aircraft are established on the localizer. Per 5-9-7(a)(1).

items.front: When may precision runway monitor approaches be conducted?
items.back: Only when the approach title is charted as PRM and the instrument approach charts specifically authorize simultaneous approaches.

items.front: When must communications transfer to the tower be complete during SOIA operations?
items.back: Complete the transfer before losing the required vertical separation between the aircraft.

items.front: What is SOIA?
items.back: Simultaneous Offset Instrument Approaches — parallel runways with centerlines at least 750 feet and less than 3,000 feet apart. Per 5-9-9(a).
